arc in
order: 1) Brahma, 2) Vidhata (according to Parasara, Brhaspati),
3) Vistju, 4) Yama, 5) Moon, 6) Kartikeya, 7) Indra (Muni says
Para.), 8) Vasus, 9) Serpent (Pisaci), 10) Dharma, ll)Rudra, 12)
Sun, 13) Manmatha, 14) Kali (Rudra), 15) ViSvedevas, and 30)
Pitarah (Parasara makes no difference between the Full and New
Moon regarding their deities). The Tithis are further divided into
five classes as I)Nanda—the First, Sixth and Eleventh Tithis, 2)
Bhadra—2nd, 7th, and 12th, 3)Jaya—3rd, 8th and 13th,4) Rikta—
4th, 9th and 14th, and5) Purna—5th, lOthand 15th. Sage Parasara,
however, gives separate names to the ten Tithis beginning with the
6th as Masa, Mitra, Mahabala, Ugrasena, Sudhanva, Sunanda,
Yama, Jaya, Ugra and Siddhi. It is to be understood that though
all the Tithis are based on the phases of the Moon, yet they are
not alike in effect. Some are beneficial, some moderate and others
harmful in the ease of auspicious functions. Each Tithi has been
